summary: Joel Spolsky recounts his frustrating three-day attempt to install discussion board software for CityDesk beta testers. After trying multiple packages — Discus, IdealBB, Manila, and Lusenet — each with significant drawbacks ranging from buggy code to excessive complexity, he gave up and wrote his own simple discussion board in two hours using ASP and VBScript. Along the way, he articulates his design philosophy: for a discussion board serving mostly casual users, learnability and simplicity should trump power-user features. The post doubles as a critique of how difficult server software installation remains compared to desktop software.
key_points: Server software installation is far harder than desktop software due to permissions, dependencies, database servers, and configuration — and neither free nor commercial software solves this well.
When designing for casual or occasional users, learnability and simplicity must take priority over power-user features and usability optimizations.
Most existing discussion boards inherit from BBS culture and are cluttered with features that serve power users while alienating novices.
Requiring membership to post is enough to turn away 90% of casual visitors who might otherwise contribute.
Sometimes building a minimal custom solution is faster and more effective than wrestling with existing packages that don't fit your needs.
money_quotes: Installing server software is just much harder than installing Windows software.
In designing a UI for anything, the very first question you always want to ask is: who is the user?
For casual users, learnability and simplicity are more important than usability and power.
All of these features are neat for power users but they just clutter up the interface for novices.

It's not uncommon for a program to start out simple and evolve to be more complicated, and you can do this without hurting 'average' usability because your users are getting more experienced on average.
Manila requires membership to post a message, and in my experience that is enough to turn away 90% of the casual visitors who might otherwise use the discussion board.
I realized that the software I want consists of exactly one table, and I could write the thing myself in less time than it took me to install some of these packages.
key_insight: When your users are casual and occasional, ruthlessly prioritize simplicity over features — and if no existing tool embodies that philosophy, building your own minimal solution may be faster than fighting the wrong tool.
